title: Bible Presbyterian Church

infobox.value: [Evangelical Presbyterian Church](/wiki/Evangelical_Presbyterian_Church_(1961) "Evangelical Presbyterian Church (1961)"), American Presbyterian Church, Faith Presbytery, Bible Presbyterian Church

is an [American Protestant](/wiki/Protestantism_in_the_United_States "Protestantism in the United States") denomination in the [Reformed tradition](/wiki/Reformed_tradition "Reformed tradition"). It was founded by members of the [Orthodox Presbyterian Church](/wiki/Orthodox_Presbyterian_Church "Orthodox Presbyterian Church") over differences on [eschatology](/wiki/Christian_eschatology "Christian eschatology") and [abstinence](/wiki/Christian_views_on_alcohol "Christian views on alcohol"), after having left the [Presbyterian Church in the United States of America](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_in_the_United_States_of_America "Presbyterian Church in the United States of America") over the rise of [modernism](/wiki/Liberal_Christianity "Liberal Christianity").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Confessional Presbyterian denomination located primarily in the United States

For the denomination in Singapore, see [Bible-Presbyterian churches (Singapore)](/wiki/Bible-Presbyterian_churches_(Singapore) "Bible-Presbyterian churches (Singapore)").

| Bible Presbyterian Church | |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Classification | [Evangelical](/wiki/Evangelicalism "Evangelicalism") [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") |
| Orientation | [Orthodox](/wiki/Conservative_Christianity "Conservative Christianity") |
| Theology | [Reformed](/wiki/Calvinism "Calvinism") |
| [Polity](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_polity "Ecclesiastical polity") | [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterian_polity "Presbyterian polity") |
| Origin | 1937  [Collingswood, New Jersey](/wiki/Collingswood,_New_Jersey "Collingswood, New Jersey") |
| [Separated from](/wiki/Schism "Schism") | [Orthodox Presbyterian Church](/wiki/Orthodox_Presbyterian_Church "Orthodox Presbyterian Church") |
| Separations | [Evangelical Presbyterian Church](/wiki/Evangelical_Presbyterian_Church_(1961) "Evangelical Presbyterian Church (1961)"), American Presbyterian Church, Faith Presbytery, Bible Presbyterian Church |
| Congregations | 28 |
| Members | 3,500 |
| Official website | <https://bpc.org/> |

The **Bible Presbyterian Church** is an [American Protestant](/wiki/Protestantism_in_the_United_States "Protestantism in the United States") denomination in the [Reformed tradition](/wiki/Reformed_tradition "Reformed tradition"). It was founded by members of the [Orthodox Presbyterian Church](/wiki/Orthodox_Presbyterian_Church "Orthodox Presbyterian Church") over differences on [eschatology](/wiki/Christian_eschatology "Christian eschatology") and [abstinence](/wiki/Christian_views_on_alcohol "Christian views on alcohol"), after having left the [Presbyterian Church in the United States of America](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_in_the_United_States_of_America "Presbyterian Church in the United States of America") over the rise of [modernism](/wiki/Liberal_Christianity "Liberal Christianity").

The denomination comprises 28 churches, 27 in the United States and 1 church in [Alberta, Canada](/wiki/Alberta,_Canada "Alberta, Canada") divided between the Great Western Presbytery, the Eastern Presbytery, the Great Lakes Presbytery, and the Florida Presbytery. The highest governing body is the Synod.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bible_Presbyterian_Church&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Origin

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bible_Presbyterian_Church&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Origin")]

The Bible Presbyterian Church (BPC) was formed in 1937, predominantly through the efforts of such [conservative](/wiki/Religious_conservatism "Religious conservatism") [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterian "Presbyterian") [clergymen](/wiki/Clergy "Clergy") as [Carl McIntire](/wiki/Carl_McIntire "Carl McIntire"), [J. Oliver Buswell](/wiki/J._Oliver_Buswell "J. Oliver Buswell") and [Allan MacRae](/wiki/Allan_MacRae "Allan MacRae"). [Francis Schaeffer](/wiki/Francis_Schaeffer "Francis Schaeffer") was the first minister to be ordained in the new denomination (1938). The First [General Synod](/wiki/Presbyterian_polity "Presbyterian polity") of the Bible Presbyterian Church was held in 1938 in [Collingswood, New Jersey](/wiki/Collingswood,_New_Jersey "Collingswood, New Jersey").

The [Bible Presbyterian Church](http://www.bpc.org) (BPC) and the [Orthodox Presbyterian Church](/wiki/Orthodox_Presbyterian_Church "Orthodox Presbyterian Church") (OPC) split in 1937. The two groups were previously (in 1936) known as the Presbyterian Church of America (not to be confused with the similar but later [Presbyterian Church in America](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_in_America "Presbyterian Church in America")). The name had to be abandoned because of a successful lawsuit in civil court by the mainline denomination regarding name infringement – a trademark-violation issue. After leaving the mainline [Presbyterian Church in the United States of America](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_in_the_United_States_of_America "Presbyterian Church in the United States of America") (PCUSA), considerable dissension became apparent among the conservative separatists themselves, and it became evident that two separate denominations were forming. The OPC became identified by Reformed Orthodoxy, and a predominance of amillennial eschatology and leadership of professors at Westminster Seminary. The OPC held to a fundamentalism in doctrine but did not identify with the cultural fundamentalism that characterized the BPC and much of the evangelical world at that time. Machen tried to mediate between the two groups, but he fell ill and died in 1937.

The BPC espoused a conservatism that showed a keen interest in cultural and [political](/wiki/Politics "Politics") matters, Communism in particular. The BPC saw the missions of the PCUSA as largely bankrupt due to the influence of Communism on the missionaries in China. [Pearl S Buck](/wiki/Pearl_S._Buck "Pearl S. Buck"), a pulitzer prize winner, was a Presbyterian Missionary and member of the mainline mission board. She had to resign from the mainline missions agency for comments in favor accommodating communism in China. She denigrated what Machen's conservatives stood for. The foreign missions leaders and J Gresham Machen hated what the mainline missions had become, seeing them as nothing more than liberal humanitarianism. Since the BPC men were in charge of Machen's Mission Board, they saw the mission of [Independent Board for Presbyterian Foreign Missions (IBPFM)](/wiki/Independent_Board_for_Presbyterian_Foreign_Missions "Independent Board for Presbyterian Foreign Missions") as more integral to the reformed witness than did the OPC. The BPC maintained a strong commitment to foreign missions as part of their commitment to the Great Commission. McIntire was defrocked alongside J Gresham Machen for his refusal to resign from the IBPFM. For decades afterward McIntire voiced his opposition to Communism and promoted conservative politics in conjunction with his BPC church planting efforts. The IBPFM is still an agency of the BPC.

Two main issues made the existence of these factions within the OPC evident. One issue had to do with a conflict over the use of [alcoholic beverages](/wiki/Alcohol_(drug) "Alcohol (drug)"). The OPC condemned drunkenness, but nevertheless did not agree that Scripture required Christians to totally abstain from drinking alcoholic beverages, while the BPC (according to the prevailing conservative cultural view of Prohibition) asserted that the Bible prohibited the consumption of alcohol entirely (see also [Christianity and alcohol](/wiki/Christianity_and_alcohol "Christianity and alcohol")). The BPC of the 21st Century holds to the same biblical view as the OPC that alcohol is permitted in moderation.

The second issue was over eschatological liberty. The Bible Presbyterians were concerned about a growing movement to scrutinize ministers with a [premillennial](/wiki/Premillennialism "Premillennialism") eschatology. Premillennialism stems from a literal reading of Matthew 24, Daniel 7, and Revelation 20. The amillennialism of the OPC is a less-literal reading which often takes eschatological passages figuratively. On one side the BPC ministers thought liberalism came from a loose reading of Scripture. And on the other the OPC sensed a theological error that could lead to liberalism. Both sides sensed a growing disunity. Though the IBPFM, the mission agency that caused the original split with the PCUSA, was chaired and run by premillenarists, missions was not the main concern of the theologians at Westminster Seminary.

Another aspect of this eschatology conflict is the commitment to reformed [covenant theology](/wiki/Covenant_theology "Covenant theology") versus the toleration of [dispensationalism](/wiki/Dispensationalism "Dispensationalism"). Those on the BPC side had come to tolerate, and some used, the popular [Scofield Reference Bible](/wiki/Scofield_Reference_Bible "Scofield Reference Bible"), whose notes taught a theological system called dispensationalism. Covenant theology is the main view held by most Reformed churches. Scofield's notes were under considerable criticism by faculty members of [Westminster Theological Seminary](/wiki/Westminster_Theological_Seminary "Westminster Theological Seminary"), who led the OPC. The OPC considered the dispensational form of premillennialism a serious error. Many in the BPC saw the criticisms against Scofield's notes as a swipe against [historic premillennialism](/wiki/Historic_premillennialism "Historic premillennialism") itself. Many more churches joined the BPC in 1938. One contributing factor of the exodus was the failure of Milo F. Jamison, a dispensationalist, to be elected Moderator of the General Assembly. The Bible Presbyterian Church has always maintained the unity of the covenant of grace (a decidedly non-dispensational position), and, in later years, passed resolutions against dispensationalism in its annual Synod meetings. The BPC is now in formal fraternal relations with the OPC, and many of these theological differences have been resolved.

### First split

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bible_Presbyterian_Church&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: First split")]

From 1955 to 1956, a fairly acrimonious split occurred in the Bible Presbyterian Church, resulting in the Bible Presbyterian Church Collingswood Synod and the Bible Presbyterian Church Columbus Synod.

While the Bible Presbyterian Church Collingswood Synod remained under the influence of McIntire, the BPC Columbus Synod, which included such men as Francis Schaeffer and [Jay E. Adams](/wiki/Jay_E._Adams "Jay E. Adams") would eventually move beyond its Bible Presbyterian Church heritage and eventually would take the name the [Evangelical Presbyterian Church](/wiki/Evangelical_Presbyterian_Church_(established_1956) "Evangelical Presbyterian Church (established 1956)") in 1961 (which is not to be confused with the [current denomination of the same name](/wiki/Evangelical_Presbyterian_Church_(United_States) "Evangelical Presbyterian Church (United States)"), founded in 1981). In 1965, the Evangelical Presbyterian Church merged with the [Reformed Presbyterian Church, General Synod](/wiki/Reformed_Presbyterian_Church,_General_Synod "Reformed Presbyterian Church, General Synod"), a denomination of "new light" [Covenanter](/wiki/Covenanter "Covenanter") descent, to form the [Reformed Presbyterian Church, Evangelical Synod](/wiki/Reformed_Presbyterian_Church,_Evangelical_Synod "Reformed Presbyterian Church, Evangelical Synod") (RPCES). The RPCES would eventually merge with the Presbyterian Church in America in 1982.

Shortly before the split, the Bible Presbyterian Church had established [Covenant College](/wiki/Covenant_College "Covenant College") and [Covenant Theological Seminary](/wiki/Covenant_Theological_Seminary "Covenant Theological Seminary"), both of which would be supported by the BPC Columbus Synod/Evangelical Presbyterian Church and both would follow the Evangelical Presbyterian Church into first the RPCES and then the PCA.

### Second split

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bible_Presbyterian_Church&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Second split")]

The remaining synod retained the name "Bible Presbyterian Church." In the 1970s, a group of churches split from the denomination, espousing a premillennial eschatology. In addition, the separate churches split for advocating [exclusive psalmody](/wiki/Exclusive_Psalmody "Exclusive Psalmody") and [abstinence from alcohol](/wiki/Abstaining "Abstaining").[[1]](#cite_note-REL-1)[[2]](#cite_note-HIS-2) This denomination, known as the American Presbyterian Church, has remained small since its formation. In 2022, it was composed of 2 churches, which together had 60 members.[[3]](#cite_note-CONG-3)[[1]](#cite_note-REL-1)

### Third split

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bible_Presbyterian_Church&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Third split")]

On March 28, 2008, the South Atlantic Presbytery voted by a wide margin to disassociate from the Bible Presbyterian Synod, in opposition to formal relations recently established between the Synod and the Orthodox Presbyterian Church. The presbytery took the name Faith Presbytery, Bible Presbyterian Church.

### Recent history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bible_Presbyterian_Church&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Recent history")]

Today the North American body has 33 congregations. Bible Presbyterians do not have synod-controlled boards for missions and education, but annually approve independent agencies for mission work, as well as colleges and seminaries.

### Resources

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bible_Presbyterian_Church&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Resources")]

The BPC publishes and maintains for public use the [Westminster Shorter Catechism website](http://www.shortercatechism.com).

## Doctrine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bible_Presbyterian_Church&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Doctrine")]

The [Westminster Confession of Faith](/wiki/Westminster_Confession_of_Faith "Westminster Confession of Faith"), [Larger Catechism](/wiki/Westminster_Larger_Catechism "Westminster Larger Catechism"), and [Shorter Catechism](/wiki/Westminster_Shorter_Catechism "Westminster Shorter Catechism") was adopted in the first Bible Presbyterian Synod in 1938. The Synod adheres to the 1788 American Revision of the Westminster Confession.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The denomination describes itself as an [evangelistic](/wiki/Evangelism "Evangelism") and [confessional](/wiki/Confessionalism_(religion) "Confessionalism (religion)") church which believes in historic [Calvinism](/wiki/Calvinism "Calvinism").[[5]](#cite_note-5) It holds to the [five fundamentals](/wiki/Fundamentalist-modernist_controversy#The_Doctrinal_Deliverance_of_1910_(the_Five_Fundamentals) "Fundamentalist-modernist controversy") and describes itself as being opposed to [modernism](/wiki/Liberal_Christianity "Liberal Christianity"), compromise, [indifferentism](/wiki/Indifferentism "Indifferentism"), and worldliness.

## General Synod

| * [All Reformed denominations](/wiki/List_of_Reformed_denominations "List of Reformed denominations")   1. **[^](#cite_ref-note_n0_6-0)** This denomination is the result of a merger between [Lutheran](/wiki/Evangelical_Synod_of_North_America "Evangelical Synod of North America"), [German Reformed](/wiki/Reformed_Church_in_the_United_States "Reformed Church in the United States"), [Congregational](/wiki/National_Council_of_the_Congregational_Churches_of_the_United_States "National Council of the Congregational Churches of the United States") and [Restorationist](/wiki/Christian_Connection "Christian Connection") churches and is such considered by some to no longer be a "Reformed denomination". 2. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-note_n1_7-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-note_n1_7-1) [***c***](#cite_ref-note_n1_7-2) [***d***](#cite_ref-note_n1_7-3) [***e***](#cite_ref-note_n1_7-4) [***f***](#cite_ref-note_n1_7-5) This refers to the denomination's heritage and not necessarily to the language in which the services are conducted in. 3. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-note_n5_8-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-note_n5_8-1) [***c***](#cite_ref-note_n5_8-2) [***d***](#cite_ref-note_n5_8-3) [***e***](#cite_ref-note_n5_8-4) [***f***](#cite_ref-note_n5_8-5) [***g***](#cite_ref-note_n5_8-6) [***h***](#cite_ref-note_n5_8-7) [***i***](#cite_ref-note_n5_8-8) [***j***](#cite_ref-note_n5_8-9) [***k***](#cite_ref-note_n5_8-10) Member of the [NAPARC](/wiki/North_American_Presbyterian_and_Reformed_Council "North American Presbyterian and Reformed Council"). 4. **[^](#cite_ref-note_n2_9-0)** This is a reformed synod within the [United Church of Christ](/wiki/United_Church_of_Christ "United Church of Christ") that is distinct in heritage, doctrine and practice from the rest of the denomination. 5. **[^](#cite_ref-note_n3_10-0)** Although Presbyterianism itself originated in [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"), those denominations stand out as having a more prominent Scottish heritage and/or connection with Scottish Presbyterian denominations. 6. **[^](#cite_ref-note_n4_11-0)** Those denominations allow member churches to be more diverse as regards the reformed tradition that they adhere to. | |
